To: J. Park
From: PC
Subject: Re: CCB minutes draft — feedback before sending

Hi J,

Thanks for sending this. First CCB minutes solo is a real thing — appreciate you being thorough. Below is what I would change before it goes out. Genuinely think you have got the substance; the issues are around format and a few attribution things that matter more on CCB minutes than weekly ones.

1. Format. The chapter I have been using as a reference (Ch 12) treats minutes as three sections: Decisions, Actions, Notes (only when necessary). The draft mixes discussion narrative into the per-CR entries, which works for capturing flavour but isn't really what minutes are for. I would restructure each CR entry as:

   - The decision itself (one to three sentences, attributable, dated)
   - Actions arising (one named owner, due date, status)
   - Notes (only if needed for future readers)

   The "team had a great discussion", "really interesting", "got quite heated" type language can come out. CCB minutes are formal documents — they'll be referenced later in closure and potentially in audit, and neutral phrasing holds up better than evaluative.

2. Actions specificity. A few of the actions need tightening:

   - CR-031: "Someone to follow up with R. Singh on the timeline" — needs a named owner and a due date. Best guess from the room — F. Demir or L. Hassan?
   - CR-032: "TBD — whether this goes to steering" — this should not go out as TBD. Either escalate to L. Hassan now to confirm, or capture as a follow-up with an owner and due date.
   - CR-033: "TBD — start date for the new hours" — same. Resolve before sending.

3. Attribution. Two things I would change:

   - CR-031 — "the team agreed that the benefits outweighed the risks" — but K. Olafsson raised security concerns. That's not unanimity. Better: "Approved. Concerns raised by K. Olafsson regarding [specific] have been added to the risk register for tracking."
   - CR-033 — "Everyone was on board" — same issue if Y. Mbeki (absent) was meant to weigh in on ops impact. If approval was unanimous among those present, say so; if absent attendees still need to weigh in, that's not a clean decision yet.

4. F. Demir's growth comments: take that out. Minutes are a record of decisions and actions, not a team morale update. Even if it's nice, it doesn't belong in the formal record.

5. Action tracking and TBDs. For the TBDs specifically: before sending, ping L. Hassan to resolve the steering-ratification question (one Slack message) and ping R. Singh to confirm the support-hours start date. That gets both TBDs out of the document. Going forward, worth having a single action tracker (separate from the minutes) that lists all open CCB actions with owner and due date, reviewed at the top of the next CCB.

6. Timing and distribution. Five days is late — the standard is 24-48 hours. For this one, I'd send today with a brief apology line at the top ("Apologies for the delay — minutes from last Wednesday's CCB; for review and any corrections by Friday"). For distribution, I would include P. Nakamura and Y. Mbeki even though they were absent — they should see the decisions. R. Singh as the vendor should also receive the CR-031 and CR-033 entries that affect his work.

If anyone challenges anything after it goes out, the move is neutral and evidence-based — your meeting notes are the record; corrections go out as an explicit "Updated minutes — change to X" note, not a silent edit.

Send me a revised version when you have it; I can spot-check before it goes out.

PC
